Why You Shouldn't Call hasOwnProperty Directly (and What ESLint Wants Instead)

ESLint has a rule that yells at you for calling hasOwnProperty directly on an object. At first it feels overly strict, but there's a real reason behind it. This post walks through why obj.hasOwnProperty('key') can break, how an object property can shadow the prototype method, and why Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call is the safer way to go.
Auto-Collapsing the Bootstrap RWD Navbar Dropdown on Click
By default, Bootstrap's RWD navbar dropdown doesn't auto-collapse on click. Here's a small trick to make it collapse properly — without breaking nested dropdowns.
Setting the Correct Build Path for Vue CLI and Vite

This post covers how to properly set the build path for Vue CLI and Vite. It actually matters a lot — if you don't set it right, both your local dev and post-build paths will break. So here's how to do it correctly.
Expose a Local Server to the Internet With ngrok (Windows + macOS Setup)

ngrok is a handy way to expose a local dev server to the internet — roughly 5 minutes of setup gets you a public HTTPS URL that forwards to your localhost. The first-run setup has a few quirks (especially on macOS — Gatekeeper blocks the binary; PATH needs configuring), so this post walks through Windows + macOS setup end to end. Useful for sharing a local prototype, testing webhooks, or wiring up a Line Bot without deploying.

Gmail Hidden Tips You Probably Don't Know

Most of us use Gmail to sign up for services — GitHub, AWS, Heroku, and so on. This post covers two underused Gmail features that make signup and sending safer: the plus-suffix alias trick (yourname+something@gmail.com) for managing per-service signups and spotting leaks, and Gmail's Undo Send delay so you can claw a badly-sent email back.
